items.title: Safety fears spark calls for better protections for Neil the seal

children.content: The elephant seal has been attracting onlookers in southern Tasmania as well as a growing social media audience. But fears for his safety and concerns that some people aren't keeping their distance have led to calls to better protect Neil.

gallery.altText: An elephant seal known as Neil the Seal lies on a beach with people watching him from a safe distance.
A woman, photographed from behind, holds her phone and photographs Neil the Seal on a beach.
A man, photographed from behind, wears an orange vest with "Marine Conservation Tasmania" written on the back.

thumbnail.altText: An elephant seal known as Neil the Seal lies on a beach with people watching him from a safe distance.
A woman, photographed from behind, holds her phone and photographs Neil the Seal on a beach.
A man, photographed from behind, wears an orange vest with "Marine Conservation Tasmania" written on the back.

items.title: 'They're often not heard': Hip hop helping kids find words for the feelings

children.content: A Tasmanian youth outreach program is providing free hip hop writing workshops for young people, which experts say could be "life-changing".

image.altText: Kieran's hand as he writes on white paper, resting on a brown table. He wears a purple hoodie and his hand has tattoos.

items.title: Tasmania moves to ban 'disgraceful' non-consensual sharing of sexual imagery

children.content: Tasmania catches up with other jurisdictions with plans to make it an offence to share sexually explicit images including "revenge porn" and AI-generated deepfakes.

image.altText: Two women stand together in a park, smiling.

items.title: The greyhound races Tasmanian authorities don't want you to see

children.content: If you watched greyhound race videos on Tasracing's website, you would likely never know that dozens of dogs were injured or killed during events last year.

items.title: What household debt levels tell us about the rise of One Nation

children.content: The paradox of a strong economy and stock market with very low consumer sentiment is not close to being resolved. Quite the opposite, the disconnect is, if anything, widening.

items.title: As its people dig, suspicion distracts Venezuela's government

children.content: Instead of focusing all its efforts on coordinating a widescale rescue operation, the Venezuelan government has been accused of hindering the response, and as has become apparent, our ability to cover it.

items.title: Albanese poised to sign major new treaty with Fiji

items.title: Fire damage to 10 shops after arson ram raid in Melbourne

items.title: 'Oldest continually practised medicine' being passed on to students

children.content: Aboriginal Australians have used native plants as bush medicine for tens of thousands of years. On Larrakia land, medical students are learning how nature can heal.

articles.title: How NAIDOC grew from a one-day protest to a week-long celebration
